data.title: Microsoft: Worth Eyeing This Dip, But Massive Risk Remains (Upgrade)
data.description:
So far in a volatile market in 2026, the dominance of large-cap tech stocks in determining the market’s fate appears to be fading. Microsoft’s (MSFT) latest earnings may be providing the clearest indication that investors are tiring of sky-high multiples and overblown AI investments.

Despite reporting strong fiscal Q2 (December quarter) results and a robust outlook for the company’s cloud products, shares of Microsoft fell 10% post-earnings, wiping out more than $300 billion in market value for the company. The stock has now essentially reversed out all of its 2025 gains. Is it a good time to wade into this dip, or has Microsoft’s long-overdue correction just begun?

I last wrote a "Sell" article on Microsoft in December, when the stock was trading just shy of $500. I’m not the type of investor that digs my heels in on any particular buy/sell call or position: everything is relative to price, especially in a stock market like today's, where reasonably valued assets are few and far between. Now in the low $400s, and considering recent cloud strength, I’m more open to exploring a buy on Microsoft, though with a number of risks still littered on the near-term horizon, Microsoft remains a mixed bag for me. I’m upping my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

The good news: Software and Azure strength

Let’s first start with the positive indicators that I think favor Microsoft. First, there has been a general narrative over the past year that the era of enterprise software may be coming to an end. The idea that “software is eating the world” and that there will be applications to improve all corporate processes has been replaced by the notion that agentic AI will soon take over most white-collar tasks and bypass the need for most software.

As far-fetched as I find this claim, it’s difficult not to acknowledge the impact that this fear has had on software stocks, taking big hitters like Salesforce (CRM), ServiceNow (NOW), and Atlassian (TEAM) down substantially over the past year. Microsoft had long seemed to be more impervious to this trend, until the most recent earnings print.

The great news: software revenue for Microsoft is still robust. As shown in the chart below, Microsoft’s Office 365 commercial cloud revenue still grew at a 14% FX-neutral y/y clip, barely decelerating from 15% growth in Q1. Seat growth of 6% y/y didn’t slow at all, which to me debunks the notion that AI and headcount reductions are having a deep impact on enterprise software seat counts. LinkedIn appears to be a continuously effective CRM tool for companies, accelerating to 10% y/y constant currency growth. And beyond just enterprise, consumers seem as dependent as ever on Excel, Word, and PowerPoint, with consumer cloud revenue growth of 27% y/y accelerating two points sequentially and more than tripling over the prior-year Q2’s growth rate of 8% y/y. All of these data points, to me, suggests that the notion that “software is dead” is far overblown.

Microsoft also continues to post excellent growth trends for Microsoft Azure, its all-important cloud infrastructure segment that is a direct competitor to AWS and Google Cloud. Azure revenue of 38% y/y (constant currency) also didn’t decelerate versus 39% in Q1, and the company’s guidance calling for 37%-38% Azure growth doesn’t suggest any slowdown is imminent either.

These two segments, software and Azure, are Microsoft’s golden goose, and strong growth trends here indicate a solid and sticky customer base that will keep generating healthy profits with which to pay for Microsoft’s capex ambitions.

The risks: OpenAI reliance, capex, and waning business lines

That said, it’s far from a perfect story for Microsoft - and perhaps it’s the presence of various risks plus investors’ nervousness about valuations that caused Microsoft to drop like a rock post-earnings.

Something else that Q2 revealed in stark numbers was the sheer reliance that Microsoft has on OpenAI, which goes far beyond its ownership stake. Microsoft’s commercial backlog is expanding exponentially - but just like fellow large-cap software provider Oracle (ORCL), much of this backlog boost is due to a single customer: OpenAI.

In Q2, Microsoft’s RPO more than tripled y/y to $625 billion. For context, the company is expected to do $328 billion in revenue this year, so its committed, contractual backlog already reflects almost two full years of revenue.

That said, Microsoft also disclosed that a staggering 45% of this open commercial backlog is from OpenAI (note that Microsoft does NOT disclose this concentration as a regular quarterly metric).

The company’s future growth hinges substantially on OpenAI’s ability to survive, generate probability, and honor its contacts. Given intensifying competition among LLMs, this is far from a given. A recent Microsoft deal with Perplexity in the amount of $750 million is a good step in diversification, but is nowhere near significant enough to offset any losses if OpenAI scales back its >$300 billion backlog.

Meanwhile, Microsoft is spending heavily on capex to meet its future commitments to OpenAI. Capex of $37.5 billion in Q2 alone rose 65% y/y and consumed a staggering 46% of revenue.

It's probably needless to say that Microsoft is now under immense pressure to show that this capex spending can yield true revenue and profit. And with so much of this buildout in anticipation of a single customer who dominates roughly half of the backlog, there is tremendous risk involved.

One final headwind to note: though it's out of the spotlight, Microsoft's non-software businesses are trailing. Windows devices revenue flattened to zero growth, from 6% in Q1. Xbox declined to -6% y/y in constant currency, demonstrating the heightened competition from the launch of rival Nintendo Switch 2. And finally, Search revenue also slowed down to 9% y/y growth, from the ~20%s just a few quarters before, highlighting Microsoft's historically poor positioning in search, now complicated by the rise of AI-powered browsers like Perplexity.

Valuation and key takeaways

We also need to acknowledge that Microsoft's blunted valuation does put it at a more attractive level vis-a-vis other tech peers. Wall Street is expecting $17.12 in pro forma EPS this year (+26% y/y) and $19.04 in FY27 (+11% y/y); so at current share prices near the $430s, Microsoft sits at just a 22.8x forward P/E. Its previously vast premium against the S&P 500 has shrunk to virtually in-line, and now sits closer to the middle of its peer set.

I don't see any near-term catalysts that can vault Microsoft higher, hence why I recommend staying on the sidelines for now as investors digest the added risk. That said, Microsoft is at least worth adding to your watch list.

data.title: AI‑Driven Trade Boosts Growth In Asia, While Singapore And Australia Near Policy Shifts
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific

Asia ends 2025 on a firm footing

The latest growth indicators suggest that Asia ended 2025 on a strong footing, particularly on the manufacturing and trade fronts, indicating that the region continues to benefit from the AI-related trade cycle. However, there was notable divergence: South Asian economies such as Vietnam, India, and Thailand recorded stronger PMI manufacturing readings compared with North Asian economies, including China and Taiwan.

Asia FX performance so far this year has also reflected this growth gap, with currencies like the Singapore dollar and Malaysian ringgit showing relative resilience, supported by stronger domestic demand prospects.

Singapore and Australia approach key policy turning points

With inflation pressures broadly contained across the region – Australia being the main exception – we continue to expect further rate cuts from the Reserve Bank of India and Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, both of which still operate at historically high real rates.

Singapore and Australia are the only two economies where policy bias has tilted toward potential tightening, though our base case for Singapore is no change. In Singapore, while CPI inflation has picked up, it remains within the Monetary Authority of Singapore's (MAS's) target band. Given heightened geopolitical uncertainty, we think MAS is unlikely to tighten preemptively. We discuss Australia’s developments in more detail below.

Volatility spreads from JPY rates into EM Asia, though bright spots remain

The volatility in JPY rates has spilt over into emerging markets, with Korea and Thailand experiencing the most significant pressures within Asia. We also expect bond markets in India and the Philippines to outperform, especially in an environment where rising fiscal and geopolitical risks since the start of the year are driving greater volatility and differentiation across Asia.

Importantly, both India and the Philippines have bond markets that are largely domestically driven, with scope for policy rates to move lower and with fiscal consolidation efforts offering an additional layer of support.

Australia: labour strength raises odds of policy tightening

Across Asia, the standout story has been Australia, where an exceptionally strong labour report for December and higher-than-expected CPI inflation for 4Q has meaningfully shifted market expectations, with markets now pricing in two rate hikes in 2026.

In December 2025, total employment growth rose to an eight-month high, with more than 80% of the increase coming from full-time employment. The unemployment rate declined by 20bp to 4.1% in December, bringing the fourth-quarter average to 4.25%, below the Reserve Bank of Australia's (RBA’s) forecast of 4.4%. More importantly, the latest inflation reading suggested persistent price pressures through 3Q and 4Q. Services inflation has remained elevated and increasingly structural, rather than transitory, keeping inflation well above the RBA’s 2–3% target range.

Taken together, these developments strengthen the case for further tightening. We now expect the RBA to raise the cash rate by 25bps in February. At the same time, GDP growth of 2.1% in 3Q is far from overheating, and recent high-frequency indicators have been mixed. This suggests that if the RBA does proceed with a February hike, it is likely to be a cautious one, reflecting the delicate balance between supporting growth and containing stubbornly high inflation.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Microsoft Vs. Meta: A Tale Of Two AI Cities
data.description:
It wasn’t all that long ago that Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) was the big tech AI king.

With an early mover advantage and its investment in OpenAI (OPENAI), shares of Microsoft were quickly snapped up by investors throughout 2023 and 2024, as seemingly prescient moves by CEO Satya Nadella positioned the company for long-term growth in a strong position in the nascent AI market.

At one point, Microsoft appeared ready to challenge Alphabet/Google (GOOG) for dominance across a number of software categories, including chatbot, search, productivity, and more.

At that point, Meta (META) was also primarily seen as an AI "also-ran," with middling reviews for the company’s Llama 3 open-source models.

Over the last few quarters, though, it appears as though the tides have begun to turn.

Microsoft, for all of its early moves, seems to have failed—thus far - at monetizing AI at anything more than an incremental pace. Of course, the company has significant AI-linked revenues, especially in cloud, but it remains unclear how much value products like Copilot are bringing to the Microsoft Office and Windows experience.

Meanwhile, Meta’s AI advances have driven substantially improved economics across the company’s entire FOA advertising model. With better AI content recommendation algorithms and stronger AI tools for advertisers, Meta has seen a significant uplift in revenue growth and other KPIs.

How did this happen? And where are these stocks headed next?

Today, I’ll break down recent moves in Microsoft and Meta post-earnings and make the case that the current trends - both positive for Meta and negative for Microsoft - are likely to continue into the foreseeable future.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Meta's AI Push

In case you missed it, it’s been an interesting earnings week in the markets.

While hundreds of prominent companies have reported their earnings in recent days, my eye has been on two firms specifically: Microsoft and Meta.

After reporting earnings on Wednesday afternoon, Microsoft saw shares dip by roughly 10%. At the same time, Meta, another one of the “Magnificent Seven” stocks, reported and saw shares rocket higher by roughly 10%:

While each of these moves makes sense in isolation, when compared directly, they paint an interesting picture about the future of AI.

Let’s start with Meta, the clear winner on Thursday.

In the company’s Q4 earnings report, Meta reported strong 24% year-over-year revenue growth.

While operating income growth did dwindle to the 6% mark - a significant drop from 38% just two quarters before - much of the increased operating costs have been driven by ongoing R&D investments:

This includes things like talent acquisition but also additional resources that are important for model development, infrastructure, and more.

This combination is what I like to see when investing, especially for long-tail growth stories: strong gross profit margins, significant top-line revenue growth, and robust investments in R&D.

In this case, those investments have produced some income lumpiness, but I don’t expect that to result in lower-for-longer net profit margins that ultimately hurt investors.

Clearly, the market doesn’t either, given the 10% rally we’ve seen.

On the product side, Meta produced strong revenue growth that was primarily the result of AI enhancements to the company’s overall consumer attention and advertising network.

While DAPs increased slightly - from 3.35 billion to 3.58 billion over the last 12 months - much of the company’s actual revenue growth came from higher user engagement, ad pricing, and ad load. In other words, growth is coming from users engaging with Meta platforms for longer on average, as content recommendation algorithms have been improved via AI models:

We're also working on merging LLMs with the recommendation systems that power Facebook, Instagram, Threads and our ad system.

Our world-class recommendation systems are already driving meaningful growth across our apps and ads business, but we think that the current systems are primitive compared to what will be possible soon...

Not only that, but Meta’s marketplace is two-sided, and AI has had an impact on advertisers as well.

On the targeting side, Meta’s models have gotten so good that many advertisers at this point have simply given Meta control over ad targeting. Historically, media buying and getting in front of the right audience was an important task delegated to proficient marketers. However, it seems as though AI has abstracted much of the importance of this process away.

This saves advertisers time and money and makes Meta an even more attractive place to advertise:

Our ads today help businesses find just the right very specific people who are interested in their products. New Agentic shopping tools will allow people to find just the right very specific set of products from the businesses in our catalog. We're focused on making these experiences work across both our feeds and across business messaging, significantly increasing the capabilities of WhatsApp over time.

Not only that, but Meta’s AI tools also allow for rapid testing and validation of ad creative, which reduces the need, especially for small companies, for design teams and other specialized personnel:

Taken together, the AI improvements to both sides of Meta’s advertiser ecosystem have driven better content recommendations to to a growing user base that sees better ads that are more targeted toward their interests. Given the size and scope of Meta’s existing flywheel, AI’s impact on the company has been huge.

Not only that, but during the earnings call, management said it sees continued opportunities to improve these models, which could unlock significant incremental value from the existing user base, while also optimizing the internal company workflows:

The last thing that I want to mention is that I think that 2026 is going to be the year that AI starts to dramatically change the way that we work...

So to do this, we're investing in AI native tooling so individuals at Meta can get more done, we're elevating individual contributors and flattening teams. We're starting to see projects that used to require big teams now be accomplished by a single, very talented person... if we do this, then I think that we're going to get a lot more done.

Overall, it’s easy for investors to draw a direct line between Meta’s AI investments and material financial impact on the company.

Microsoft's Modest AI Gains

For Microsoft, it’s been a tougher road.

On Wednesday afternoon, the company reported Q2 2026 earnings, which showed revenue growth of 16.7%.

While respectable, this growth rate pales in comparison to Meta.

Some of this growth differential comes down to sheer size. Over the last 12 months, Meta produced roughly $200 billion in revenue, while Microsoft produced roughly $300 billion. Everyone knows it is more difficult to grow a larger revenue base at faster rates given the sheer math that comes with scaling.

That said, some of the growth differential is also down to Microsoft’s relative failure to efficiently monetize AI product improvements.

Unlike Meta, Microsoft does not offer a massive family of social networks that it can enhance directly with AI.

Instead, it has three core product areas, including cloud, productivity, and personal computing. While many of these have network effects, none of them are self-reinforcing flywheels.

As such, the company has more limited incremental ways of introducing and boosting AI revenue.

Firstly, in the company’s Intelligent Cloud segment, revenues grew 29% year over year. As the second-largest cloud service provider in the world, Microsoft Azure is a huge business, and the introduction of better AI models and new data center capacity for AI workloads has boosted pricing and utilization from customers:

In many ways, this is the clearest and most straightforward AI case for Microsoft.

That said, at only $33 billion in revenue, cloud makes up roughly 40% of Microsoft’s overall revenue.

Additionally, it recently came out that OpenAI is a massive contributor to RPO, creating significant customer concentration risk for Microsoft.

Thus, Microsoft’s AI cloud growth is credible, but perhaps not as robust as advertised.

In my view, the other two business segments, including productivity and personal computing, also have a much more tenuous link to AI growth factors.

On the personal computing side, revenue growth actually came in at -3%, as personal spending on Windows products decreased by roughly $400 million year over year.

While this loss was driven by weakness in the company’s Xbox hardware and services division, growth in search and news advertising revenue was up only 7%:

To me, this shows that a resurgent Bing may not be in the cards for Microsoft, especially as competitors like Google have materially improved their AI search offerings.

On the productivity and business side, the company’s largest segment, revenues, increased 16% year over year:

While this is relatively impressive, Microsoft 365 commercial cloud revenue grew roughly 17%, primarily driven by growth in revenue per user, not user growth.

In my mind, this shows that aside from its cloud business, one of Microsoft’s only routes to driving serious AI revenue growth is through its productivity service offerings, but it has not yet been able to meaningfully lift revenue per user at more than an incremental pace.

Additionally, the lack of significant new user growth here (only 6%) shows that Copilot offerings - across Office, Windows, and GitHub - are not a significant enough draw to attract new businesses. Percentage growth in copilot seats has been high, but only 15 million seats have converted, and the offering isn't valuable enough to be driving in-line new business.

Anecdotally, I’ve tried Microsoft’s Copilot products a number of times and have been consistently disappointed with the user experience, overall integration with other Microsoft products, and quality of results.

So much for the OpenAI model/investment moat.

To be clear, I’m not saying that Microsoft is a bad company. In fact, Meta and Microsoft are two of the best companies in the world. Despite my, and the market’s, relative disappointment with Microsoft’s AI growth, both companies are structurally profitable tech behemoths that I don’t see going anywhere anytime soon.

Valuations

But how are each of these companies priced?

Actually, quite similarly.

At present, Microsoft trades at a price-to-sales ratio of roughly 10.5 and a price-to-earnings ratio of roughly 27.1:

Meta, for its part, trades at a price-to-sales ratio of 9.4 and a price-to-earnings ratio of roughly 31.4:

On a purely trailing earnings basis, Meta is actually the more expensive stock.

Of course, before Meta’s 10% rally and Microsoft’s 10% decline, this wasn’t the case, but both stocks are functionally priced about the same right now, at least on a trailing basis.

One important wrinkle is that with stronger expected earnings-per-share growth in the coming years, Meta’s PEG ratio is only 1.25 versus Microsoft’s 1.8:

Thus, when you take growth into account, Meta might still be a bit cheaper than Microsoft.

Which is the Better Buy?

Overall, it raises the question: which stock would you rather own?

For me, the answer is clear: Meta.

Make no mistake, Microsoft is an excellent business that churns out tens of billions of dollars every year in income and produces some of the most dominant software products in history. Some might look at the stock’s recent decline and point to the fact that Microsoft is now priced well versus its five-year average.

However, I believe that Microsoft’s average price over the last few years has been unreasonably elevated due to its AI halo, and that its current fair value is probably somewhere close to the current price.

Looking forward, I believe Meta has a much stronger trajectory for continued growth.

Unlike Microsoft, AI improvements impact nearly all of Meta’s revenue pie, and management commentary anticipating strong, continued model improvements suggests the potential to juice near- and medium-term revenue growth rates.

As AI capex tapers in the coming years, I expect Meta’s operating margins to reinflate and even potentially surpass pre-AI levels.

Taken together, Meta represents a strong investment package at the moment.

Good luck out there~

data.title: Deckers: HOKA Is Still Sprinting Forward
data.description:
Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the October-December period on the 29th of January. The footwear company reported better-than-expected growth momentum with both of its core brands, sending the stock up by 14% in post-market action as of writing. Brand momentum concerns have been dodged for now as HOKA is strong across the board. UGG’s momentum is still flashing some weakening signals for upcoming quarters.

I upgraded the stock’s rating to Buy in my previous October 2025 article, titled “Deckers: Q2 Sell-Off Created A Buying Opportunity.” The stock has since returned 29%, outperforming the S&P 500’s small 2% gain.

Deckers Q3 Financial Review: Great Across the Board

As suggested in the introduction, Deckers’ fiscal Q3 report came in above expectations. Revenues came in at $1.96 billion with good year-on-year growth of 7.1%, beating Wall Street’s consensus by $89 million. By brand, HOKA’s revenue grew by 18.5% to $629 million, and UGG’s revenue grew by 4.9% to $1305 million. The last months of the year are seasonally strong for UGG. Remaining brands’ revenue plummeted by -55.5% to $23 million as Koolaburra’s standalone operations are being phased out, but the investment case is clearly based on Deckers’ two core names.

Deckers’ growth was consistent across sales channels at 8.1% and 6.0% in direct-to-consumer and wholesale, respectively. Good growth in the direct-to-consumer channel signals healthy brand demand, while wholesale revenue has less signaling value over the short term due to quarterly variance in shipments. Both core brands’ channel growth was balanced according to Q3 earnings call remarks; consumer-level brand demand seems to have stayed good.

Margins remained healthy—Deckers managed to maintain great full-price selling, leading the gross margin to only show 50 basis points of pressure to 59.8% despite a challenging tariff landscape. Led by Deckers’ good SG&A management, operating income improved by 8.3% to $614 million. The quarter’s $3.33 EPS beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.57.

Bernstein and Piper Sandler analysts, among others, have previously expressed concerns about especially HOKA’s brand momentum amid industry discounting, tightening competition in running shoes, and shifting fashion cycles. A return to double-digit growth isn’t likely due to the aforementioned factors, and Q3 results did show a slowdown in growth, but the report still eased concerns about Deckers’ brands’ momentum as margins remained resilient and growth was healthy.

Analysts have been especially cautious of Deckers’ U.S. performance. Growth was again driven by international markets at 15.0%, but U.S. revenues stabilized with 2.7% growth. A saturated U.S. market hasn’t stopped Deckers’ growth completely in the market, being a major positive in the report, in my opinion. Deckers notes that HOKA’s direct-to-consumer sales returned to healthy growth in the U.S. market during Q3 and that the brand gained market share.

Deckers still has significant distribution expansion potential with HOKA, especially in Europe and China, creating room for more long-term growth.

The Outlook is Great for Hoka, Slower for UGG

The FY2026 revenue guidance mid-point was raised by $62.5 million to $5400-5425 million due to slightly improved growth expectations for both HOKA and UGG, and the operating margin guidance was raised by 100 basis points to 22.5% due to better-than-expected gross margins. The raised mid-point still only implies revenue of $1060 million for Q4, suggesting much slower 3.7% year-on-year growth. Deckers does have a habit of guiding revenue conservatively, though; the company has now beaten Wall Street’s revenue consensus 17 times in a row. The next report seems likely to be the 18th in my opinion.

HOKA has started the quarter with good brand momentum in January, according to the search volume indicator. Expanding brand recognition and distribution in international markets and stabilization in the U.S. continue to carry high expectations for the brand. Deckers’ FY2026 guidance implies 13-14% growth for the brand in Q4.

UGG’s momentum is slowing down; the brand’s revenue is expected to be flat in Q4, and while Deckers notes a negative wholesale shipment impact, the brand’s search volume has continued to show moderation from September 2025 forward. Recent product launches, including the Quill franchise, have turned out successful, but slowing brand momentum poses a threat to growth over the longer term. Deckers expects UGG to continue growing in FY2027, but I believe that expectations need to be increasingly cautious for the brand.

There’s Upside Left in the Investment

I revised my discounted cash flow (DCF) model expectations for Deckers. I raised FY2026 expectations as Deckers raised the company’s guidance for the year but kept revenue growth expectations nearly the same afterward as HOKA’s outlook is even greater but UGG’s outlook is moderating. The FY2025-FY2035 CAGR is now set at 5.3% with 2.5% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

Better gross margin stability has improved Deckers’ margin outlook, in my opinion. While the company may still have to resort to higher discounting in upcoming years, I now expect the EBIT margin to stabilize at 22%. The cash flow conversion should remain good.

The estimates put Deckers’ fair value at $143.0, suggesting 25% upside as of writing. There’s still clear upside in the investment as HOKA has dodged concerns about slowing brand momentum, and as Deckers has maintained great pricing power.

The fair value estimate is up from $126.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.31%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t expect Deckers to draw any debt. For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.27% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.93. With a liquidity premium of 0.15%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 8.31%.

Takeaway

HOKA and UGG were both strong in Q3 across markets and sales channels. Most notably, HOKA’s U.S. direct-to-consumer performance stabilized, and Deckers’ pricing power remained great, leading to record quarterly earnings. The overall outlook is good for Q4 and beyond but is increasingly split between a strong HOKA and a weaker UGG. As there’s still upside in the investment, I remain at a Buy rating for DECK stock.

data.title: AppFolio: A Great Chance To 'Buy' The Dip As Multiples Compress (Upgrade)
data.description:
As we move deeper into the Q4 earnings season, the pervading mood in the markets appears to be one of caution. Investors are very wary of escalating macroeconomic and trade tensions, which look perilous set against the backdrop of steep valuations.

And yet, this period of caution may be a good time to buy many previously expensive stocks on a dip, especially those that are still delivering strong growth. AppFolio, Inc. (APPF) is one such stock. The real estate software giant just slipped more than 10% after reporting Q4 results, with investors responding tepidly to the perception of a weak guide for 2026. With the stock now down more than 25% over the last year, we have to ask ourselves, is now the right time to "Buy" the dip?

I last wrote a neutral article on AppFolio in November, when the stock was last trading above $210. With AppFolio now receding to the low $180s post-earnings, my tune on this stock is certainly changing. At these levels, we’re more attuned to the many positives that AppFolio boasts: a strong recurring revenue base, clear upsell trends, and sticky clientele. I’m upgrading the stock to "Buy."

Let’s first kick off the discussion with the elephant in the room: AppFolio’s guidance. For FY26, the commercial real estate company guided to revenue of $1.10-$1.12 billion, or 17% midpoint growth versus $951 million in FY25 revenue. It’s also expecting 25.5-27.5% pro forma operating margins, the midpoint of which reflects 180bps of y/y margin expansion.

Wall Street’s consensus models had called for $1.13 billion in revenue. To me, the ~1% miss in top-line guidance is absolutely not in proportion with the stock’s >10% post-earnings drop. Also, good context is the fact that for FY25, originally AppFolio had guided to $920-$940 million in revenue, and actually achieved $951 million - 1% higher than the original high end of its guide. AppFolio may well achieve beyond Wall Street’s targets this year, as it sets the bar low for a beat-and-raise cadence this year. It’s a good opportunity, in my view, to scoop up AppFolio at a more modest valuation.

At current post-earnings share prices near $183, AppFolio trades at a market cap of $6.55 billion. After we net off the $328.3 million of cash on AppFolio’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $6.22 billion. This represents a 5.6x EV/FY26 revenue multiple: not a bad price at all for a Rule of 40 software stock. We note that in the past, AppFolio had once commanded double-digit revenue multiples.

Use this dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Download

Let’s now go through AppFolio’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

AppFolio’s revenue grew 22% y/y to $248.2 million, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $246.6 million (+21% y/y) by a one-point margin. Also notable is the fact that revenue growth actually accelerated one point versus 21% growth in Q3, which is an indicator of very strong demand despite supposed macroeconomic headwinds.

In fact, those headwinds for the housing market may be acting as tailwinds for AppFolio. Sky-high housing costs and high mortgage rates have sidelined many would-be buyers, which is creating a massive pool of longer-term renters. This, in turn, has made commercial real estate lucrative, and has emboldened AppFolio’s core customer demographic.

The company's number of customers increased 6% y/y, while the total number of units managed on the AppFolio platform grew 8% y/y to 9.4 million, with the increase in average units per customer indicating AppFolio's growing trend toward larger-scale commercial landlords. Meanwhile, upselling also continues to be a core revenue driver for the company. The company notes that the proportion of commercial customers on the higher-tier Plus and Max subscriptions has topped 25%, with the company noting strong upsell momentum among both smaller SMB customers as well as higher-tier, upmarket customers.

The company's value-added services portfolio also continues to expand. This is where the majority of AppFolio's growth lies. $722 million, or about three-quarters of full-year revenue was from commercial landlords purchasing add-ons; value-added revenue grew at a 19% y/y pace in FY25. The latest offering, managed building Wi-Fi services, is seeing strong traction. Per CEO Shane Trigg's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

With Resident Onboarding Lift, co-created with Second Nature, we've delivered a seamless move-in experience that activates resident services and drives new revenue opportunities.

Our newest service within Resident Onboarding Lift is group rate Internet, allowing property managers to offer their residents fully managed high-speed Internet at an attractive rate. Resident Onboarding Lift and our other products and services connect back to one goal, delivering measurable performance to our customers. We recently asked new customers who switched to AppFolio about their experience. 96% said switching to AppFolio has improved their overall business performance. 94% said AppFolio has improved their resident satisfaction.

Outcomes like these directly contribute to strengthening our position as the preferred platform in real estate. AppFolio has been named the overall leader on the G2 Grid, recognition based on the feedback of our customers and their firsthand experiences with our platform.

On the profitability front, AppFolio achieved 470bps of y/y pro forma operating margin expansion in Q4, up to 24.9%. As previously mentioned, the company is aiming for roughly two points of full-year operating margin expansion in 2026.

We note that with the company planning for 17% growth on top of ~26% pro forma operating margins, AppFolio remains well within the Rule of 40.

Key Takeaways

In today's market, we have to be incredibly careful with what and when we buy. AppFolio is timed well, with valuation multiples that are well below other high-teens/~20% growth peers despite the stock's Rule of 40 orientation. AppFolio is an unmatched product in the real estate segment, with sticky and loyal customers. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: AppLovin: Your Golden Buying Opportunity Ahead Of Earnings (Preview)
data.description:
Investment Rating: “Buy”

AppLovin Corporation (APP) is a software-based platform for advertisers that initially operated across Advertising and Apps but then sold the Apps business and focused entirely on developing its Advertising segment because the launch of AXON 2.0 promised to deliver higher ROI over time. The stock price made a massive surge over the past 5 years, growing from about $60/share to over $740/share at some point. As I'm writing these lines, APP is trading at $569/share after its YTD correction of ~16%.

The company is approaching its earnings release in 2 weeks (February 11th), and I believe the current sentiment of the market might abruptly change and provide a great opportunity for dip-buyers as the company beats the consensus again, guiding for a strong 2026 thanks to the continued adoption of their new offerings among clients (I'm talking about their AXON Ads Manager launched in October 2025). For the level of growth that should come in the next few years (both in terms of the sales and earnings per share), APP is priced at a discount, in my opinion. I've calculated a meaningful upside potential for the next 12-24 months, and it makes me bullish on AppLovin for the medium term.

From Niche Leader To Global Ad-Tech Challenger

AppLovin's AXON 2.0 engine runs proprietary machine learning algos, processing trillions of data points in order to make close-to-real-time predictions for a better matching of advertisers and publishers (through automated auctions). It sounds complicated, but in a nutshell, this engine helps APP stand out among other digital ad platform providers because, through the quality of its prediction system, it allows advertisers to hit specific return on ad spend (ROAS) targets with quite high precision.

AppLovin utilizes its own first-party data for AXON 2.0, so its capability of performing highly granular optimizations is something that is usually impossible to achieve for its peers, who have to use lossy third-party data. This particularity, or better called competitive advantage, helped APP basically quadruple the ad spend on its platform in just 2 years, and the growth isn't slowing down, as far as we can see it from the latest results.

I strongly believe that the level of growth that AppLovin has managed to achieve isn't going away in 2026-2027.

They launched AXON Ads Manager on October 1, 2025, so it's been only a few months since it saw the light. The early data sends us optimistic signals—the overall spend from self-service advertisers went up by 50% w/w in the early weeks. So, with more APP's customers getting used to the new platform (it takes time according to the management's commentaries), the current massive customer base of ~1 billion DAU is likely to boost the top line throughout the year. Specifically for the upcoming quarter, the management guided for 12-14% QoQ growth in revenues, so we won't have to wait for too long before the factual success of AXON 2.0 and AXON Ads Manager contributes to the revenue growth acceleration.

After their portfolio of first-party mobile games divestment, the business structure has been simplified, and the company is going to be judged as an elite enterprise software platform going forward thanks to its above-average margins.

I should note here that the current price action—the YTD correction—seems to be mainly dominated by the recently published short report:

But the management has already rejected all the capital structure issues raised, asserting full compliance with the SEC.

I think the negative sentiment is likely to change over time—that's my base expectation. Especially so if the company keeps delivering on the management's guidance and continues to expand.

Let's Preview APP's Upcoming Q4

As you could have seen above, the adjusted EBITDA margin in Q3 2025 amounted to ~82% (vs. already solid 77% in Q3 2024), and the trend remains upward-sloping in nature. I don't expect a sudden drop in margins in the upcoming Q4 report. The management guided for a range of 82-83% on the adjusted EBITDA level.

In the fourth quarter of 2025, we anticipate revenue between $1.570 billion and $1.6 billion, reflecting between 12% and 14% sequential growth. With adjusted EBITDA between $1.290 billion and $1.320 billion, targeting an adjusted EBITDA margin of 82% to 83%.

Source: APP's earnings call (Q3 2025).

In Q2, they were expecting a margin of 81% (no range) for Q3, so the factual Q3 metric beat the management's guidance by about 100 basis points. It's a very strong beat that is directly translating to EPS growth, helping APP beat the key headline figure (albeit slightly).

The key difference now lies in revisions. After the Q2 beat, analysts were aggressively bullish on the nearest quarters, lifting estimates by 10-30% in some cases. After the modest beat of less than 3% in Q3, they were less enthusiastic, lifting the consensus figures only for far-end forward years.

The quarterly revisions have been basically silent for the past month, and the consensus EPS estimates have gained only 2-3% over the past 3 months (compare it to the 6 months below).

It has become easier for APP to beat on the bottom line this time, in my view, especially if we assume the firm is able to repeat its Q2 success and reach an above-guided EBITDA margin.

AppLovin's Growth Is Worth The Price Tag

As I've already mentioned above, the growth at AppLovin isn't stopping. According to Seeking Alpha, APP has managed to reach a 5-year CAGR of over 34% in terms of revenues, and its net loss of $125.2 million turned to a net profit of over $2.8 billion (on a TTM basis). For the next 5 years, the EPS and revenues CAGRs are expected to be at 25.9% and 30.2%, respectively.

For this level of growth, the market is pricing the stock at less than 39x FY2026 earnings and less than 30x FY2027 earnings.

We have to recall here that APP is translating over 80% of its revenues into adjusted EBITDA, and most of it then transfers into FCF. These profitability levels are hard to compare with when we try to find a market peer.

I assume that APP, for the level of its superior profitability, should be valued at a PEG of 1. Even if they don't beat the FY2025 EPS at all, following the Q3 path (when they barely beat the consensus on EPS), the stock would appear cheap at that PEG ratio.

PEG = price-to-earnings / EPS growth YoY

if PEG = 1, EPS growth = 56.63%, and EPS = $14.64 (all consensus), then:

1 * $14.64 * 56.63 = $829.06/share

The current stock price is ~$569.24, so the upside potential that I've just arrived at points to 45-46% for the next 12-24 months.

Risks To My Views

My thesis is based on the core assumption that AppLovin will keep growing its revenues and EPS over the next years at least at the consensus levels. But if the actual growth in 2026-2028 turns out to be lower than what the market is pricing in—and we see that in Q3 the positive surprise was very low—the market is unlikely to give APP 40-45x in earnings multiple. Macro risks and the criticality of the ads market may contribute to that. In this case, the upside that I calculated in this article will become misleading and wrong.

Conclusion

AppLovin and its highly efficient, highly profitable business model stand out among tech peers when we compare forward growth to forward multiples. I like that the Q4 expectations are not pricing in excessive premiums, making it easier for AppLovin to beat more meaningfully compared to what we saw in Q3. The company reports its Q4 results on February 11th, in less than two weeks, and I think the dip it's going through is giving investors a nice buying opportunity.

data.title: Danaher Stock Plunge Does Not Represent A Good Entry Point
data.description:
Jan. 28 was a pretty painful day for shareholders of Danaher Corporation (DHR). Shares of the stock dropped 4.8% after management announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Although it looks as though 2026 will be positive for the company, and even though the company exceeded analysts’ estimates when it came to revenue and adjusted earnings per share, GAAP earnings per share fell slightly short of expectations. Even after this sizable decline in share price, the stock is outperforming since I called it a "Hold" back in September 2025. Units are up 15.1%, comfortably outperforming the 7.4% jump experienced by the S&P 500.

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I would say that things are going well. Revenue, profits, and cash flows are all rising nicely. Leverage for the company is in check. And relative to other similar firms, shares are actually attractively priced. But unfortunately, right now, the stock is fairly pricey on an absolute basis. That is what has prevented me in the past from upgrading it to something more bullish. And even after improved financial performance and a decline in share price that occurred because of the data provided by management for the quarter, the stock is not cheap enough on an absolute basis to justify a change at this time.

Danaher Shares Are Too Pricey for My Liking

For the most part, I would say that the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a pretty good time for shareholders of Danaher. The company reported revenue of $6.84 billion. This was up nicely from the $6.54 billion the company reported a year earlier. And it happened to be $28.4 million above what analysts were hoping to see. With this rise in revenue came higher profits as well. Earnings per share popped from $1.49 to $1.66. That was unfortunately $0.01 below what analysts were hoping to see. But it still translated to a rise in net income from $1.09 billion to $1.20 billion.

When it comes to adjusted earnings per share, the picture was better than expected. The company saw a jump from $2.14 to $2.23. That ended up being $0.04 greater than what was anticipated. And it meant a rise in adjusted net profits from about $1.56 billion to $1.61 billion. Other profitability metrics improved without exception. Take operating cash flow as an example. It increased from $2.02 billion to $2.12 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get an increase from $1.85 billion to $1.97 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $2.12 billion to $2.13 billion. The chart above also shows results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. Although we did see net profits and operating cash flow contract, all the other metrics included in that chart showed growth year over year.

All the company's operating segments experienced growth during this time. Take, for instance, the star of the show. This was none other than its Biotechnology segment. Revenue of $2.03 billion exceeded the $1.87 billion reported the previous year. This rise of 8.8% was driven in part by foreign currency fluctuations. But even if we remove that from the equation, we would have gotten around a 6% increase in core sales growth for the year. Management continues to push sales higher through innovations. For instance, during the quarter, the company benefited from Cytiva’s launch of over 20 new products across the biologics workflow, new 500- and 2,000-liter formats of the Xcellerex X-platform bioreactor. It also launched two new Protein A resins. With this rise in sales, we also saw a nice jump in operating profit from $508 million to $540 million. But the overall margin for the company did contract slightly from 27.2% to 26.6%. That still makes it the most profitable part of the company from a margin standpoint. However, the Diagnostics segment is definitely catching up.

Year over year, it reported a rise in revenue of 3.1%, climbing from $2.64 billion to $2.72 billion. Unfortunately, core sales growth was slightly lower at 2%. The rise in sales here stemmed at least in part from the company’s Beckman Coulter Diagnostics business as it expanded its Dxl 9000 assay menu. The firm specifically singled out progress in neurodegenerative diseases such as those involving first-to-market automated, high-throughput BD-tau research-use-only immunoassay. It also continued to expand its cardiac and blood virus menus. There were other wins as well. But not all of them occurred during the quarter. About a week ago, Cepheid received FDA clearance for its Xpert GI Panel, which is a multiplex PCR test that can detect 11 common gastrointestinal pathogens using just one sample. That is hugely innovative if you ask me. While the revenue increase for this segment was modest, especially when we strip out changes in working capital, the move higher in profitability was meaningful. Segment operating profits shot up from $624 million to $713 million, with the overall operating profit margin expanding solidly from 23.7% to 26.2%.

Lastly, we have the Life Sciences segment. This part of the company saw a rise in revenue from $2.03 billion to $2.09 billion. Management continues to stress that biotechnology demand remains stable. The real contribution to the growth that the segment saw, then, involved what management described as a ‘modest’ recovery when it comes to the pharmaceutical space, with Europe being a bright spot for the business. However, in the United States and China, demand for academic and research activities is lower. And that is helping to drive down that segment’s consumables business, specifically as there is lower demand for plasmids and mRNA. Funding pressure across early-stage biotech and academic research is also problematic. It's not surprising, given these developments, that operating profits for the segment would actually contract from $376 million to $336 million.

On the whole, I am happy with what I saw here. And what's even better is the fact that management seems at least somewhat optimistic when it comes to 2026. Management expects, for instance, for core sales growth this year to be up by between 3% and 6%. This would be mostly driven by a 6% rise associated with the Biotechnology segment. As revenue grows, we should expect adjusted earnings per share to climb as well. The firm is currently targeting earnings of between $8.35 and $8.50. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of about $6.04 billion. And if we scale up other profitability metrics accordingly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $7.69 billion and EBITDA of $8.29 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2024 and 2025, we can easily see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are very lofty multiples. I know that this space has a tendency to trade at rather high prices. But as a value investor, seeing a company trade in excess of 20 times cash flow is difficult to digest, especially if revenue and profit growth is only good instead of great. Of course, some investors will disagree with me. And that's fine. Their argument will likely hinge on the fact that, relative to other similar firms, the stock is actually a bit cheap. In the chart below, you can see what I mean here. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, Danaher is cheaper than any of the five institutions I stacked it up against. But because of the net debt picture of the business, the EV-to-EBITDA approach puts it closer to the middle of the group, with two of the firms being cheaper than it is.

Speaking of net debt, in the chart below, you can see the overall trajectory of the company when it comes to net debt and the net leverage ratio over the last few years now. You can also see how the company stands up against the same five institutions I compared it to already. With a reading of 1.80, the company actually has greater leverage than three of the five competitors. This is not awful by any means. But at the same time, I wouldn't exactly say that it is justification for some sort of premium compared to the competition. If anything, perhaps a small discount compared to those firms that have less leverage than it does would be appropriate.

Takeaway

The data available right now shows me that Danaher is doing a fine job. But as a value investor, I have to be principled. The fact of the matter is that while the stock is cheaper than most other similar firms, it is still expensive on an absolute basis. This year, the picture should look even better than it did last year. And normally, you would expect the market to respond accordingly. However, the move lower is almost certainly attributable to the fact that the market recognizes how pricey the stock is. And even with financial performance that was, for the most part, positive, pushing shares higher just did not make sense. For now, I feel comfortable with my "Hold" rating on the company. But as time goes on, I will evaluate it based on new data that's available.

data.title: AMD: Facing Its Moment Of Truth
data.description:
I first upgraded Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) to a “BUY” in Q2 2025, arguing the company would greatly benefit from a new AI hardware cycle. The stock has performed well since then, more than doubling in price.

AMD’s bull run continued through Q2 and Q3 results, despite both of these quarters being somewhat soft, with the company growing at a far slower pace than its main rival NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

With Q4, I think the time for excuses has run out. AMD needs to show their chips are gaining traction and fully benefitting from AI demand and the significant CapEx investment tied to it.

Why AMD will face anagnorisis with Q4 earnings

Anagnorisis, or in plain English “moment of recognition,” is a moment in a play when a character makes a critical discovery. I think AMD will go through such a moment during Q4 earnings on February 3rd.

The company launched its MI350 series of chips in 2025, with production only ramping up in the second half of last year. I think Q3 earnings, the last available at the time of writing, did not yet capture in full the results of MI350 chips due to production ramping up. Despite this, the company did well that quarter (recovering from a disappointing Q2) and grew revenue at its record pace of 36% year-over-year.

For Q4, I expect to see further acceleration in revenue growth driven by MI350 sales, which to me represents the core of the bullish thesis with this company.

Markets are forward looking, and with a P/E of above 60 at the time of writing, there is not a lot of room for error. In this sense, AMD has no more excuses and needs to show traction for MI350 to justify its valuation and grow further.

If things go well… CapEx spooked markets, but AMD is a beneficiary

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) just had one of its worst days on the stock market following Q4 earnings, due to an increase in CapEx and a somewhat soft growth for its cloud division. Something similar happened to Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) in November, again due to markets reacting to increased CapEx guidance for the future.

AMD, however, should be a beneficiary of this wave of AI-driven CapEx by major Tech companies. Neither Microsoft nor Meta nor any other Tech company has so far signalled they will reduce their CapEx plans. If anything, markets were spooked by the exact opposite: an increase in these plans.

In the context of Q4 earnings, I think AMD has no more excuses: since demand for chips has only grown in Q4, the company needs to show MI350 can deliver.

A bet still worth taking: who should consider buying AMD today?

I am personally very bullish on the AI race. I think it’s unlikely we are going to see a significant slow down in microchip demand. On the contrary, I recently argued that we may see an acceleration and an actual “AI bubble” forming in 2026. This is the fundamental reason why I remain bullish on AMD, even as there is a reduced margin of safety within the stock today.

My valuation model for the company, which I covered in detail in a past article, sees the company valued between $ 219 and $ 2500 + per share. The table below summarizes the assumptions behind my valuation, which is a DCF model on a 10-years time frame.

At the time of writing, AMD trades at ~$250, well above my base stock price target of $219 per share. Contrary to when I first recommended this stock, I do not think this leaves a large margin of safety to investors. If things go south in Q4, and AMD fails to convince investors about the traction of their MI350 chips, I think the company could face a significant correction (something I will cover shortly in my risk section).

However, if the AI race accelerates as I expect it to, I think AMD still has a lot of upside. Which brings me to my next point: AMD remains a compelling play for those bullish on AI, even at today’s prices. I believe semiconductors in general are a great play, as they benefit from CapEx regardless of whether AI is getting monetized immediately or not. And within semiconductors, I believe AMD is one of the best bets, a function of being the only company that today can compete directly with Nvidia, benefitting from demand that the latter cannot satisfy.

As proof of AMD being the best bet in semiconductors today, consider the company outperformed even the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (SOXL) in the last five years. This is a 3X leveraged ETF (!) that is exposed to AMD itself to the tune of ~5% at the time of writing. I recommended SOXL as a high-risk bet on AI during the correction in late Q3 last year. I expect AMD to keep beating its peers (even if not necessarily SOXL) for the foreseeable future.

If things do not go well... (risks to my bullish thesis)

At the cost of repeating myself, I will say once again that a bet on AMD today is a bet on MI350 competing with Nvidia’s chips in efficiency and AI applications, especially those related to inference.

Readers may notice that I chose not even to mention AMD’s next generation of chips, the MI450 Series. These are set to be released later in 2026 and will be crucial for the future of the company. However, my eyes are still on the MI350 story: if AMD fails to show traction in Q4, then the stock may correct deeply, and only rebound if markets are ever convinced AMD can crack its next generation of chips.

On top of AMD facing its “moment of truth” in Q4, other risks include the company being exposed to AI, the current direction of interest rates (higher rates would negatively affect CapEx in data centers) and, more generally, stock volatility.

Anyone thinking to enter AMD today should carefully consider the fact that any margin of safety is likely gone, and the stock already prices in a successful AI semiconductor player.

Conclusion: I remain bullish as we enter Agentic AI and Robotics

In much of my past work I described the idea that the AI race is unfolding in four phases:

  1. A “Picks & Shovels” phase, where GPU companies like Nvidia and AMD supply chips for all types of AI applications.

  2. A “Cloud & Early Adopters” phase where giants such as Microsoft and Meta integrate AI for efficiency in their business (improving the ROI of ads or automating their workforce) while scaling cloud platforms.

  3. An Agentic AI phase, where more applications will emerge, automating workflows in software development, sales, and finance.

  4. A Robotics phase where AI meets the real world in autonomy and humanoids—something I discussed in my latest article about Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

I think 2026 is the year of Agentic AI, with AI applications starting to have an impact on businesses (and potentially impacting the job market and starting to reshape the economy). In this context, AMD will need to prove they have what it takes to “attend the party” with their chips. The time of excuses has run out, and I see Q4 earnings as the moment of truth. I personally remain bullish on the company and its ability to capture AI demand, and reiterate my BUY rating, with a long-term view.

data.title: Fidelity Contrafund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the fourth quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 2.61%, versus 2.66% for the benchmark, the S&P 500® index. Stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace, supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. The past three months, we continued to favor fast-growing, "best of breed" firms. Importantly, given our focus, longer-term performance comparisons strongly favor the fund over the benchmark.

Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. Equities rose nonetheless, boosted by earnings momentum and a constructive expansionary backdrop. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting, as it looked to balance a recent slowdown in hiring and inflation running higher than its 2% target. The S&P 500® rose 2.34% for the month.

Momentum faded a bit in November (+0.25%), as stocks weathered the shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off that mostly reflected concern about an AI bubble. However, the focus returned to a U.S. economy largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10. The index achieved a record close on Christmas Eve before a sluggish final week resulted in a flattish gain for the month, a 17.88% rise for the year and a 38.65% increase since the low on April 8.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI, as media & entertainment stocks were up 9%.

All other groups lagged the index for the three months. Real estate (-3%) and utilities (-1%), small components of the S&P 500, each lost ground, while consumer staples finished at roughly breakeven. Materials, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks all gained roughly 1%, as did information technology, which represented 35% of the index in Q4. Financials (+2%) reflected a strong showing by banks (+7%) but weakness (-1%) among insurers, and energy (+2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil.

Security selection in the capital goods industry within the industrials sector notably contributed to the fund's performance versus the S&P 500® in Q4. This mostly reflected a sizable non-benchmark stake in Space Exploration Technologies (+89%), a private company that makes spacecraft and provides satellite communications and launch capabilities. We like SpaceX, in part, for its Starlink business focusing on low-earth-orbit satellites, which has created a web of broadband internet access to previously underserved communities in rural and remote areas. In December, investors applauded reports of the potential for an initial public offering in 2026.

In tech, largely avoiding Oracle (ORCL) contributed to relative performance. In Q4, shares of the software and cloud-services provider returned about -31%, so we made a good decision in eliminating the fund's scant exposure in November. We were skeptical about the profitability of Oracle's cloud business relative to peers, including Amazon.com (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT).

Conversely, our stock picks in media & entertainment stood out as the largest detractor versus the benchmark, especially overweights in Meta Platforms (META) (-10%), parent of Facebook and Instagram, and video-streaming service provider Netflix (NFLX) (-22%).

In October, Meta reported record quarterly revenue but net income that was much lower than expected, largely due to spending on AI infrastructure. The company said it expects total expenses to "grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026 than 2025." We like that Meta has embraced AI to improve the quality and relevance of the content and advertisements it shows its users. We reduced exposure to Meta the past three months, but the stock remained by far the top holding and overweight at year-end.

Shares of Netflix fell due to weaker-than-expected Q3 earnings and an announced plan to buy Warner Bros. Q3 revenue growth was in line with guidance, but operating margin missed due to a tax dispute with Brazil that cut into earnings. The stock fell further in December after Netflix announced the proposed Warner Bros. purchase, an acquisition that would include Warner's film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO. Investors worried the deal could face regulatory scrutiny. We lowered the fund's stake in Netflix in Q4, but it remained a large holding and overweight, based on its leadership position and the stickiness of its business.

Outlook and Positioning

As 2026 begins, the global and U.S. business cycles remain constructive, with monetary and fiscal easing in the U.S. likely to continue, in our view. Noteworthy trends in the U.S. include strong corporate fundamentals and earnings momentum, softening labor markets, and wealth-powered high-end consumption. Capital spending by corporations has experienced a powerful AI-driven upswing, although signs of broadening activity remain faint. We believe the massive tax-and-spending package that became law on July 4 provides an additional tailwind for corporate earnings growth.

The Fed cut its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25% percentage points in September, October and December, amid signs of softer employment conditions. The market and Fed expect two more rate cuts in 2026, due to expectations for soft labor markets and short-lived inflation. By December, central banks in the eurozone, the U.K. and Canada were all on pause.

The near-term outlook appears favorable for continued economic and business expansion, but we believe policy uncertainty, inflation persistence and elevated asset valuations bear watching. Uncertainty related to economic policy dropped in the second half of 2025 after hitting a record high in April, but it has remained elevated and stands in sharp contrast to stock-market volatility, which declined back to roughly its historical average.

The U.S. dollar dropped roughly 10% against major currencies in 2025, its worst calendar year since 2017. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields finished 2025 effectively unchanged at around 4.2%, completing a fairly range-bound year.

After two years of near-zero goods inflation, tariff hikes led to a rise in goods prices in the second half of 2025. Inflation remained sticky in housing and services, keeping headline consumer inflation elevated and notably higher than the Fed's 2% target.

In managing the fund, we follow the investing tenet that a stock's price follows the actual and expected earnings per share of the underlying company over time. So, as the earnings outlook for the fund's holdings changed in Q4, we made only modest adjustments.

Combining technology and communication services, the fund is overweight what we consider tech-related "growth plays." The embrace of AI by companies, governments and individuals is the biggest trend in the market, in our view. We believe AI will affect every business, so we are working closely with the Fidelity research team to identify winners and losers in this changing tech landscape. Accordingly, the fund's position in communication services, including sizable commitments to Meta, Google parent Alphabet (GOOGL) and Netflix, represented 22% of assets and was by far the top sector overweight. Meta is classified as a media name, but we view it as a leading, founder-led tech firm that has embraced AI to improve two critical parts of its business: choosing content and ads to show users, and helping advertisers create better ads.

Tech represented 26% of assets at year-end, the top sector allocation and essentially unchanged from September 30. The biggest holdings here are AI-focused chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA), software and cloud giant Microsoft, personal-electronics giant Apple, and Amphenol, which makes electronic and fiber-optic connectors.

The fund's allocation to the financials sector was about 16%, a bit lower than three months ago but still a meaningful overweight. Insurance-focused conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) anchors the fund's position in the sector, followed by much smaller investments in banking firm JPMorgan Chase and credit-card processor Visa.

We continue to seek more exposure to international stocks and small- and mid-cap firms. The latter are often new issues from the past five to seven years. We believe many of these businesses, which we call "tomorrow's blue chips," can grow faster than the largest companies in the market.

Fidelity® Contrafund® (FCNTX)

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Contrafund® is an opportunistic, diversified equity strategy with a large-cap growth bias.

Philosophically, we believe stock prices follow companies' earnings, and those companies that can deliver durable multiyear earnings growth provide attractive investment opportunities.

As a result, our investment approach seeks firms we believe are poised for sustained, above-average earnings growth that is not accurately reflected in the stocks' current valuation.

In particular, we emphasize companies with "best of breed" qualities, including those with a strong competitive position, high returns on capital, solid free-cash-flow generation and management teams that are stewards of shareholder capital.

We strive to uncover these investment opportunities through in-depth bottom-up, fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team.

data.title: Charter Communications Defies Fears Of Extinction
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications (CHTR) have been a terrible performer over the past year, losing nearly half of their value. Concerns about degradation of the broadband business, combined with its debt load, are the primary challenge facing shares. Broadband was once seen as a growth segment, immune from the cord-cutting of video, after all, we all need internet for streaming, etc. However, increased competition from nontraditional players and slow household formations have pressured this business. Strong Q4 results may temper these fears a bit, and shares surged nearly 8% in response in early trading. I have argued these fears are overblown, most recently rating CHTR a “strong buy” in November but being bullish on Charter was one of my worst calls in 2025. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit CHTR to determine whether shares can rebound in 2026 or if the pain will continue.

Cost cuts support EBITDA

In the company’s fourth quarter, Charter earned $10.34, which was $0.46 ahead of expectations even as revenue declined by 2.4% to $13.6 billion. EBITDA declined by 1.2% to $5.7 billion with the decline primarily due to lower political advertising on its local networks. Advertising was down $139 million, and this decline is entirely expected given the Presidential election last year, which boosts spending; the midterms this year should provide a boost to ad revenue.

Absent this, EBITDA would likely have been slightly higher, given the company’s very strong cost controls. Thanks to growth in mobile, Charter has been able to push one-product customers to two product relationships, though it continues to see losses of 3+ product customers, given the ongoing pressure in video, though there are hopeful signs this may be changing.

Broadband attrition is stable, video is encouraging

Broadband customers declined by 119,000, so it does remain a challenging environment for this segment; the pace of decline is better than the 177k loss a year ago. The Q4 customer loss was similar to what we have seen the prior six months. While results here are not strong, with shares so beaten up, it has allowed investors to sigh a breath of relief that the situation has not worsened. Internet customers are down 1.3% from last year. Thanks to pricing increases, revenue was up 0.7% from last year to $5.9 billion. I expect 0.2-0.7% growth in internet revenues in 2026 as price increases offset a continued modest bleed in customers.

Mobile remains a strong spot with CHTR adding 428k lines and approaching 12 million customers. Total customers are up 19% from last year, but I expect the pace of growth to moderate going forward, given it has already captured a substantial portion of its own customer base. I expect growth to narrow towards 10-12% by the end of 2026. Mobile revenue was up 13%, and it may pass $1 billion in Q1 2026 (if not then, it should in Q2). This is a small part of the business at ~7% of revenue, so its growth has a limited impact. Still, mobile is steadily becoming a more important driver of financial results.

When I last wrote about Charter, I discussed my view that after years of attrition video may be approaching its bottom, as most of the remaining customers are likely loyal to the product and CHTR’s packaging of streaming services has improved the value of its cable proposition. Evidence is mounting this may be the case. It added 44k in Q4, and its pace of annual declines has narrowed to 2.2%. Perhaps, video is not in an unstoppable decline after all.

Still, while customers are stabilizing, all is not good in this business. Video revenue was down 10% to $3.2 billion. Its added streaming offerings reduced revenue by about $125 million, or ~4%. All else equal, I do expect this business to decline, and the need to invest in more content to maintain subscribers is likely to reduce margins, but the pace of decline is improving. I expect revenue to decline mid single-digits here in 2026.

Cash flow trends are solid

Importantly, while revenue clearly has pressure, Charter is a very cash generative business. Free cash flow was $5 billion, up $700 million from last year. Declines in cap-ex should support meaningful free cash flow growth even in a flat revenue environment. In 2025, CHTR focused that free cash flow on shareholders, repurchasing $5.4 billion of stock, including $760 million in Q4. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 11% from last year.

In 2026, Charter plans to invest $11.4 billion in cap-ex. This is down about $300 million from last year as it starts to complete network expansion efforts. Importantly, we should see cap-ex continue to decline as it completes the network evolution. I remain unconvinced that cap-ex can really sustainably decline to $7.5-$8 billion. I view $9.5-10 billion as the more likely ending place, given inflation. Still, this will unlock ~$2 billion of free cash flow relative to 2025 levels. With this clear cash saving, free cash flow is likely to rise over time even if we do see ~3-5% revenue declines, a meaningful deterioration of its flat to down 1% trend.

The company ended the year with $477 million of cash and about $95 billion of debt. The absolute size of its debt load is clearly high, and this has been a source of concern for investors. However, relative to Charters’s earnings power, the debt load is sustainable. The company ended the year with 4.2x leverage, stable over the past year. After it completes its acquisition of Cox, Charter aims to bring leverage to a targeted range of 3.5-4.0x, given its larger scale and Cox’s own solid balance sheet. This will be a two-year process, and I expect leverage to remain with 0.2x of current levels through 2027. Given its fairly predictable revenue and strong free cash flow, I view this as safe place to run the business. At current levels, I would much rather see CHTR spend free cash flow on buybacks than on debt reduction.

Shares are very undervalued

Overall, Charter reported a solid Q4. We saw similar underlying trends to recent results, and the pace of pressure on the business is not worsening. Video is also becoming a source of some upside surprise. The ability to enhance the value of this product also should give comfort that CHTR is not a slave to declines in broadband either and can invest to improve the product and customer retention.

At $200, shares have a 17% free cash flow yield, which speaks to how negative sentiment has become. As discussed above, this is not a melting ice cube business, rather free cash flow is set for growth. I expect at least $5 billion again this year, and over $6.2 billion of stand-alone free cash flow in 2027. Even at a 12% free cash flow yield to protect investors against a 2% long-term decline in the business and still yield a 10% long-term return, that points to fair value in the $340 area. As such, I continue to see Charter as a strong buy.

While ongoing negativity around the business may make this convergence to fair value a very prolonged process, I see CHTR as tremendously undervalued. Sentiment is far too negative given actual cash flow trends, and I believe shares can at least recover to $300 this year. I view it as a compelling value and expect 2026 to be a much better year.
